lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: The air bites shrewdly; it is very cold.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: It is a nipping and an eager air.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: What hour now?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: I think it lacks of twelve.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: No, it is struck.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Indeed? I heard it not: then it draws near the season

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Wherein the spirit held his wont to walk. A flourish of trumpets, and ordnance shot off, within.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: What does this mean, my lord?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: The king doth wake to-night and takes his rouse,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Keeps wassail, and the swaggering up-spring reels;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And, as he drains his draughts of Rhenish down,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: The kettle-drum and trumpet thus bray out

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: The triumph of his pledge.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Is it a custom?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Ay, marry, is’t:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: But to my mind, though I am native here

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And to the manner born, it is a custom

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: More honour’d in the breach than the observance.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: This heavy-headed revel east and west

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Makes us traduced and tax’d of other nations:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: They clepe us drunkards, and with swinish phrase

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Soil our addition; and indeed it takes

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: From our achievements, though perform’d at height,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: The pith and marrow of our attribute.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: So, oft it chances in particular men,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: That for some vicious mole of nature in them,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: As, in their birth—wherein they are not guilty,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Since nature cannot choose his origin—

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: By the o’ergrowth of some complexion,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Oft breaking down the pales and forts of reason,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Or by some habit that too much o’er-leavens

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: The form of plausive manners, that these men,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Carrying, I say, the stamp of one defect,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Being nature’s livery, or fortune’s star—

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Their virtues else—be they as pure as grace,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: As infinite as man may undergo—

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Shall in the general censure take corruption

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: From that particular fault: the dram of eale

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Doth all the noble substance of a doubt

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: To his own scandal.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Look, my lord, it comes!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Angels and ministers of grace defend us!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Be thou a spirit of health or goblin damn’d,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Bring with thee airs from heaven or blasts from hell,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Be thy intents wicked or charitable,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Thou comest in such a questionable shape

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: That I will speak to thee: I’ll call thee Hamlet,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: King, father, royal Dane: O, answer me!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Let me not burst in ignorance; but tell

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Why thy canonized bones, hearsed in death,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Have burst their cerements; why the sepulchre,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Wherein we saw thee quietly inurn’d,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Hath oped his ponderous and marble jaws,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: To cast thee up again. What may this mean,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: That thou, dead corse, again in complete steel

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Revisit’st thus the glimpses of the moon,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Making night hideous; and we fools of nature

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: So horridly to shake our disposition

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: With thoughts beyond the reaches of our souls?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Say, why is this? wherefore? what should we do? Ghost beckons Hamlet.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: It beckons you to go away with it,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: As if it some impartment did desire

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: To you alone.

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: Look, with what courteous action

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: It waves you to a more removed ground:

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: But do not go with it.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: No, by no means.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: It will not speak; then I will follow it.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Do not, my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Why, what should be the fear?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I do not set my life in a pin’s fee;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And for my soul, what can it do to that,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Being a thing immortal as itself?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: It waves me forth again: I’ll follow it.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: What if it tempt you toward the flood, my lord,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Or to the dreadful summit of the cliff

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: That beetles o’er his base into the sea,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: And there assume some other horrible form,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Which might deprive your sovereignty of reason

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: And draw you into madness? think of it:

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: The very place puts toys of desperation,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Without more motive, into every brain

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: That looks so many fathoms to the sea

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: And hears it roar beneath.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: It waves me still.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Go on; I’ll follow thee.

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: You shall not go, my lord.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Hold off your hands.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Be ruled; you shall not go.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: My fate cries out,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And makes each petty artery in this body

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: As hardy as the Nemean lion’s nerve.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Still am I call’d. Unhand me, gentlemen.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: By heaven, I’ll make a ghost of him that lets me!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I say, away! Go on; I’ll follow thee.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: He waxes desperate with imagination.

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: Let’s follow; ’tis not fit thus to obey him.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Have after. To what issue will this come?

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: Something is rotten in the state of Denmark.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Heaven will direct it.

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: Nay, let’s follow him.

glosses.definition: indulges in a heavy drinking bout
percussion and horn, sounded to salute toasts
more honored by being broken than kept
the drunken reputation far and wide

glosses.phrase: pith and marrow of our attribute
glosses.definition: essence and core of our worth

glosses.phrase: spirit of health or goblin damn’d

glosses.definition: overhangs its base like an overshot eyebrow
sinew of Hercules's Nemean lion (emblem of strength)

annotations.title: The Danes as drunkards: Shakespeare's political diplomacy
annotations.body: Hamlet's discourse on Danish drinking customs ('heavy-headed revel east and west / Makes us traduced and taxed of other nations') appears only in the Second Quarto, not in the Folio. Why the cut? One reading: by 1603 James I, married to Anne of Denmark, had drawn England into closer diplomatic relations with Denmark, and Shakespeare's company suppressed a passage that called their new queen's countrymen swine. Whether or not the cut was deliberately political, the speech is the play's most sustained meditation on the 'one defect' — how a small flaw of nature can undo a whole character. That diagnostic will become Hamlet's own self-indictment by Act 4.

annotations.title: 'Spirit of health or goblin damn'd'
annotations.body: Hamlet's first address to the ghost carefully preserves the Elizabethan threefold uncertainty: he may be a holy spirit, a damned devil in disguise, or a hallucination. The phrase 'questionable shape' puts the ghost in the category of things one may legitimately interrogate. Hamlet's skepticism here is theological as much as personal — a Protestant's wariness of Catholic ghost-lore — and it prepares the ground for the 'play within a play' trap in Act 3, by which he will test the ghost's credibility. Until then, any action Hamlet might take on the ghost's word would be imprudent, possibly damnable. This is the play's first principled defense of delay.

annotations.title: The ghost's silence and the platform's edge
annotations.body: The ghost will not speak in the presence of others; it beckons Hamlet away. Horatio's warning — that the spirit may 'assume some other horrible form' or tempt Hamlet toward the cliff — is the play's first suggestion that the revenant might be diabolical. The image of the cliff that 'beetles o'er his base' would have been staged with the trapdoor and gallery architecture of the Globe: the ghost leading Hamlet 'off-stage' is physically leading him upward or downward in the Elizabethan three-tiered cosmos. The geography matters because the truth the ghost is about to deliver will depend on where it comes from — purgatorial fire, hellish fraud, or the mind's own projection.
sources: Andrew Gurr, The Shakespearean Stage 1574–1642 (3rd ed., 1992)

annotations.title: 'Something is rotten in the state of Denmark'
annotations.body: Marcellus's line is one of the most quoted in all of Shakespeare, and its iconic status tends to strip its dramatic force. Coming at the end of the scene, after the ghost has exited and Hamlet has followed against his friends' pleas, the line serves as a choric comment: the ghost is a symptom, not a cause. The word 'state' is precise and political — not 'condition' but commonwealth, body politic. The metaphor of rot implies something dead within a living organism, corruption hidden inside a ceremonial surface. Everything that will unfold — the assassination plot, the spying, the poisoning at a royal feast — is already implied in this one sentence.

trials.prompt: What is Hamlet criticizing when he describes the 'heavy-headed revel east and west'?
options: The peasant classes' drunkenness at harvest festivals.
The Danish custom of heavy drinking during royal celebrations.
Claudius's personal habit of drugging his wine.
The soldiers on the night watch, who he fears are drunk.

trials.prompt: What is Hamlet arguing in the passage about the 'vicious mole of nature' and the 'dram of eale'?
options: That no one can overcome their inherited flaws.
That a single defect can undo the virtues of an otherwise noble character.
That illness is contagious and must be quarantined.
That the Danish court is corrupt beyond redemption.

trials.prompt: Why does Hamlet address the ghost as both 'spirit of health' and 'goblin damn'd'?
options: He believes the ghost is two different spirits at once.
He is preserving theological uncertainty about whether the ghost is holy or diabolical.
He is mocking the ghost to test its patience.
He is quoting a Latin incantation Horatio taught him.

trials.prompt: What do Horatio and Marcellus fear when the ghost beckons Hamlet away?
options: That the ghost will reveal Hamlet's secrets to the King.
That the spirit may tempt Hamlet to leap from the cliff or drive him mad.
That Hamlet will be delayed from his studies.
That Claudius's spies are watching and will report them.

trials.prompt: Marcellus's closing line — 'Something is rotten in the state of Denmark' — uses which metaphor?
options: A legal metaphor: corruption as a miscarriage of justice.
An organic metaphor: hidden decay within a living body politic.
A military metaphor: an enemy within the walls.
A theological metaphor: damnation seeping into the soul.
